metadata.purpose: Consumer debt defense case law survey for multi-state law firm launch

standing.case_name: Sierra Managed Asset Plan, LLC v. Hale
standing.citation: 240 Cal.App.4th Supp. 1, 193 Cal.Rptr.3d 265 (App. Dep't Super. Ct. 2015)
standing.court: Cal. App. Dep't (Appellate Division, Super. Ct.)
standing.holding_for_defense: Debt buyer's custodian of records lacked personal knowledge of the original creditor's record-keeping practices and could not authenticate records of the original creditor; declaration did not meet the necessary foundation for the business-records exception, and therefore judgment for debt buyer was reversed.
standing.practical_takeaway: Attack the declarant's qualifications: an assignee's employee who merely received records from the original creditor lacks the personal knowledge to authenticate them under Evidence Code § 1271.
standing.notes: Hale is published authority that remains influential; distinguished but not overruled by CACH LLC v. Rodgers (2014) 229 Cal.App.4th Supp. 1.

standing.citation: 216 Cal.App.4th Supp. 1, 157 Cal.Rptr.3d 156 (App. Dep't Super. Ct. 2013)
standing.court: Cal. App. Dep't (Appellate Division, Super. Ct.)
standing.holding_for_defense: Section 98 declarations are a departure from the hearsay rule; affiant must be personally available for service within 150 miles of the courthouse and must demonstrate adequate foundation for the business-records exception. Establishes the framework for challenging CCP § 98 declarations in limited civil cases.
standing.practical_takeaway: Confirm declarant's 150-mile availability and personal knowledge before accepting a § 98 declaration; failure to satisfy either element renders the declaration inadmissible and collapses the debt buyer's evidentiary case.
standing.notes: Cited with approval in Meza v. Portfolio Recovery Associates, LLC (Cal. 2019).

standing.case_name: Meza v. Portfolio Recovery Associates, LLC
standing.holding_for_defense: CCP § 98 requires affiants to be personally available for service of process at an address within 150 miles of the place of trial for a reasonable period during the 20 days before trial; this requirement is not satisfied merely by designating a law firm's office address. An FDCPA claim may proceed where a debt buyer's § 98 declarant was not physically available for personal service.
standing.practical_takeaway: Where the debt buyer's § 98 declarant is headquartered outside California or more than 150 miles away, move to exclude the declaration and simultaneously assert an FDCPA claim for the misleading implication that in-court testimony could be compelled.
standing.notes: California Supreme Court answer to certified question from the Ninth Circuit; directly controls CCP § 98 practice statewide.

chain_of_title.citation: 229 Cal.App.4th Supp. 1, 176 Cal.Rptr.3d 843 (App. Dep't Super. Ct. 2014)
chain_of_title.court: Cal. App. Dep't (Appellate Division, Super. Ct.)
chain_of_title.holding_for_defense: A CCP § 98 declaration may be sufficient to authenticate business records under Evidence Code § 1271 even where the declarant did not work for the original creditor, provided the declarant demonstrates sufficient knowledge of the assignee's record-keeping practices; court declined to follow Hale's strict rule. Although defense-side result was mixed, the split with Hale highlights that evidentiary foundation requirements are highly fact-specific and litigable.
chain_of_title.practical_takeaway: Use the Hale/Rodgers split to argue in the trial court that debt buyers must produce a witness with personal knowledge of how the original creditor created the records — not just how the assignee stored them.
chain_of_title.notes: Creates an intra-district split with Sierra Managed Asset Plan v. Hale; both cases remain valid appellate division authority.

chain_of_title.case_name: Cal. Civ. Code § 1788.52 (Fair Debt Buying Practices Act)
chain_of_title.holding_for_defense: Debt buyers must possess six categories of information before making any written statement to collect a consumer debt, including the names of all persons who owned the debt after charge-off and the dates and amounts of any payments made. Any lawsuit requires the attached contract or charge-off statement; default judgment requires additional authenticated business records.
chain_of_title.practical_takeaway: Plead FDBPA violations as affirmative defenses and counterclaims; a debt buyer that cannot produce the statutory documentation cannot obtain a default judgment and risks dismissal of its collection action.
chain_of_title.notes: Substantive statute; confirmed by California Court of Appeal (Sixth Appellate District, March 2026) to confer statutory standing without proof of actual harm.

chain_of_title.case_name: Riverisland Cold Storage, Inc. v. Fresno-Madera Production Credit Ass'n
chain_of_title.holding_for_defense: Overruled the Pendergrass limitation on the fraud exception to the parol evidence rule; parol evidence of fraud may be admitted to contradict the terms of an integrated written contract.
chain_of_title.practical_takeaway: Where the original creditor or debt buyer made oral representations inconsistent with the written agreement — e.g., promises about billing or settlement — Riverisland allows the consumer to introduce that evidence even against an integration clause.
chain_of_title.notes: Landmark 2013 Supreme Court decision; no subsequent legislative override.

hearsay_business_records.case_name: Sierra Managed Asset Plan, LLC v. Hale
hearsay_business_records.citation: 240 Cal.App.4th Supp. 1, 193 Cal.Rptr.3d 265 (App. Dep't Super. Ct. 2015)
hearsay_business_records.court: Cal. App. Dep't (Appellate Division, Super. Ct.)
hearsay_business_records.holding_for_defense: Custodian of records for a debt buyer failed to establish the necessary foundation under Evidence Code § 1271 because the declarant lacked personal knowledge of the original creditor's record-keeping practices; records excluded as inadmissible hearsay.
hearsay_business_records.practical_takeaway: Force the debt buyer to produce a live witness with first-hand knowledge of how the original creditor created and maintained the records; a debt-buyer employee who merely received and stored documents cannot lay the hearsay foundation.

hearsay_business_records.case_name: Meza v. Portfolio Recovery Associates, LLC
hearsay_business_records.holding_for_defense: Where a debt buyer's CCP § 98 declarant is not physically available for personal service within 150 miles of the courthouse, the declaration is subject to exclusion, and filing such a declaration may constitute a false or misleading representation under the FDCPA.
hearsay_business_records.practical_takeaway: Combine a motion to strike the § 98 declaration with an FDCPA claim; each improper declaration generates a potential statutory-damages recovery.

sol.citation: Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 337; White v. Moriarty, 15 Cal.App.4th 1290, 1299 (1993)
sol.holding_for_defense: Credit card and written consumer-debt contracts are subject to a four-year statute of limitations under CCP § 337. Accrual begins when the right of action fully vests — generally the date of first default. The creditor cannot arbitrarily select the accrual date.
sol.practical_takeaway: Calculate the actual first-default date from account statements; if the suit was filed more than four years after that date, the claim is time-barred and the FDCPA is violated if the suit was brought on a time-barred debt.
sol.notes: The 4-year rule for written contracts (CCP § 337) remains settled California law; choice-of-law clauses selecting Delaware or South Dakota do not generally import shorter SOLs under California choice-of-law doctrine.

sol.case_name: Cal. Civ. Code § 1788.56 (Fair Debt Buying Practices Act)
sol.holding_for_defense: A debt buyer is prohibited from bringing suit if the applicable statute of limitations has expired; must plead and prove the SOL has not expired; must provide a mandatory disclosure to any consumer for whom the SOL may have expired.
sol.practical_takeaway: Screen every file for SOL issues; a time-barred debt buyer lawsuit violates both the FDBPA and FDCPA, generating counterclaim leverage.

default_vacatur.case_name: Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 473(b) and § 473(d); Trackman v. Kenney
default_vacatur.citation: Cal. Code Civ. Proc. §§ 473(b), 473(d); Trackman v. Kenney (2010) 187 Cal.App.4th 175
default_vacatur.holding_for_defense: § 473(b) provides discretionary relief from default on showing of mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect within six months; § 473(d) provides relief from a void judgment at any time (no statutory time limit). In Trackman, failure to serve the defendant at the correct address rendered the default judgment void.
default_vacatur.practical_takeaway: For recently-served clients, use § 473(b) promptly within 6 months with a declaration showing excusable neglect; for old judgments, § 473(d) based on improper service has no time limit following the California Supreme Court's 2024 ruling abrogating the judicially-created 2-year limitation.
default_vacatur.notes: California Supreme Court (Jenkins, J.) in 2024 abrogated the lower-court rule limiting void-judgment vacatur to two years; § 473(d) motions are now timely at any point.

default_vacatur.holding_for_defense: Defendants in debt buyer cases who did not receive actual notice in time to defend may move to set aside a default or default judgment within six years of entry of default, or 180 days of first actual notice, whichever is earlier.
default_vacatur.practical_takeaway: The FDBPA-specific vacatur window is far longer than CCP § 473(b)'s six-month window; always evaluate both grounds when a debt-buyer default judgment is discovered late.

pleading_requirements.citation: Cal. Civ. Code §§ 1788.50–1788.64 (FDBPA); Cal. Code Civ. Proc. §§ 96–98 (limited civil case disclosure)
pleading_requirements.holding_for_defense: The FDBPA requires debt buyers to attach the contract or charge-off statement to the complaint in a collection suit; CCP § 96 entitles the defendant in a limited civil case (≤ $35,000) to demand the plaintiff's witness list and exhibit list, requiring disclosure up to 30 days before trial.
pleading_requirements.practical_takeaway: Send a CCP § 96 demand for witness/exhibit list immediately after the answer is filed in every limited civil case; if the debt buyer cannot produce authenticated contract documents, move for judgment on the evidence.

pleading_requirements.holding_for_defense: Businesses that negotiate consumer contracts primarily in Spanish (or other specified languages) must provide a translation before the contract is signed; failure gives the consumer the right to rescind, and the rescission right runs against any subsequent assignee.
pleading_requirements.practical_takeaway: Ask every Spanish-speaking client whether contract discussions with the original creditor were conducted in Spanish; if yes, the contract may be rescindable under § 1632, eliminating the debt buyer's entire claim.

fdcpa_leverage.case_name: Gonzales v. Arrow Financial Services, LLC
fdcpa_leverage.holding_for_defense: Letters implying that the debt collector could report obsolete debts to credit bureaus when it could not legally do so violated both the FDCPA § 1692e and the Rosenthal Act; Rosenthal Act remedies are cumulative and available even when the FDCPA affords relief — double recovery is permissible.
fdcpa_leverage.practical_takeaway: Identify any misrepresentation about credit reporting rights or the legal status of the debt; plead both FDCPA and Rosenthal Act claims to stack potential recovery.

fdcpa_leverage.case_name: Henson v. Santander Consumer USA Inc.
fdcpa_leverage.holding_for_defense: A debt buyer that owns the debt and collects for itself is not a 'debt collector' under the FDCPA's 'regularly collects debts owed another' prong — BUT may still qualify as a debt collector under the 'principal purpose' prong if the principal purpose of its business is debt collection. This means major debt buyers (Midland, Portfolio Recovery, LVNV) qualify under the principal-purpose prong.
fdcpa_leverage.practical_takeaway: Establish at intake that the plaintiff's principal purpose is debt collection; virtually all named debt buyers satisfy the principal-purpose prong, preserving FDCPA coverage.

fdcpa_leverage.holding_for_defense: The litigation privilege (Civ. Code § 47) does not bar claims under the Rosenthal Act and FDCPA; a debt collector's unlawful conduct in connection with litigation does not gain immunity merely because it occurred in a litigation context.
fdcpa_leverage.practical_takeaway: When opposing counsel argues litigation privilege bars a Rosenthal Act claim, cite Persolve: the privilege does not insulate specific statutory violations directed at debtors.

state_counterclaim.case_name: Cal. Civ. Code §§ 1788–1788.33 (Rosenthal Fair Debt Collection Practices Act)
state_counterclaim.citation: Cal. Civ. Code §§ 1788–1788.33 (1977, as amended through 2024)
state_counterclaim.holding_for_defense: The Rosenthal Act incorporates all FDCPA requirements and extends them to original creditors; violations by debt buyers or their attorneys are actionable; class actions are available; remedies are cumulative with FDCPA.
state_counterclaim.practical_takeaway: Unlike the FDCPA, the Rosenthal Act applies to original creditors — file Rosenthal claims against banks and credit unions that have not sold the debt. SB 1286 (eff. July 1, 2025) expands the Act to certain commercial debts.

state_counterclaim.case_name: Cal. Civ. Code §§ 1788.50–1788.64 (Fair Debt Buying Practices Act — FDBPA)
state_counterclaim.citation: Cal. Civ. Code §§ 1788.50–1788.64 (eff. Jan. 1, 2014; Sixth Appellate District 2026 standing confirmation)
state_counterclaim.holding_for_defense: FDBPA violations are actionable without proof of actual harm; even technical violations (e.g., improper font size in required disclosures) give rise to statutory damages.
state_counterclaim.practical_takeaway: Screen all collection letters from debt buyers for FDBPA documentation and disclosure requirements; each violation can support a standalone statutory damages claim.

fee_shifting.citation: 15 U.S.C. § 1692k(a)(3); see also Karton v. Ari Design & Construction, Inc., 61 Cal.App.5th 734 (2021)
fee_shifting.holding_for_defense: Attorney fees are mandatory to a prevailing consumer under both the FDCPA and the Rosenthal Act; fees are computed using the lodestar method (hours × reasonable rate); California courts may adjust the lodestar upward or downward based on skill and civility factors.
fee_shifting.practical_takeaway: Maintain meticulous time records from the first day; request a lodestar multiplier in cases that required exceptional work or deterrence; never agree to cap fees in a settlement of the consumer's claim.

fee_shifting.case_name: Cal. Civ. Code § 1788.30 (Rosenthal Act fee shifting)
fee_shifting.holding_for_defense: A prevailing consumer under the Rosenthal Act is entitled to actual damages, statutory damages of $100–$1,000 for willful violations, and reasonable attorney fees. Fees are mandatory; no showing of bad faith required.
fee_shifting.practical_takeaway: File as a cross-complaint or affirmative claim in the collection action; the mandatory fee-shift makes even modest violations economically viable to litigate.

standing.case_name: Glarum v. LaSalle Bank National Ass'n
standing.holding_for_defense: An affidavit of indebtedness by a bank employee (Orsini) constituted inadmissible hearsay where the affiant had no personal knowledge of how the computer-stored data was produced and could not authenticate it; summary judgment for the bank reversed.
standing.practical_takeaway: Oppose summary judgment motions by debt buyers with hearsay objections to affidavits from custodians who cannot demonstrate personal knowledge of how the original creditor's records were created.

standing.case_name: Fla. R. Civ. P. 1.130 (Instruments Attached) — applied to debt buyer complaints
standing.citation: Fla. R. Civ. P. 1.130(a) (2001, as amended); Walters v. Ocean Gate Phase I Condominium, 925 So.2d 440 (Fla. 5th DCA 2006)
standing.holding_for_defense: A complaint based on a written instrument does not state a cause of action until the instrument or an adequate portion thereof is attached to or incorporated in the complaint.
standing.practical_takeaway: Move to dismiss any debt buyer complaint that fails to attach the underlying credit agreement or a sufficient excerpt; a bare account-number reference does not satisfy Rule 1.130.

chain_of_title.case_name: Glarum v. LaSalle Bank National Ass'n
chain_of_title.holding_for_defense: Affidavit by servicer employee without personal knowledge of how the underlying data was produced was inadmissible hearsay under § 90.803(6)(a), Fla. Stat.; the affiant must have knowledge of the creator's record-keeping practices.
chain_of_title.practical_takeaway: Challenge every summary-judgment affidavit with a hearsay objection grounded in the affiant's lack of personal knowledge of the record-creation process — not merely the record storage.

hearsay_business_records.holding_for_defense: Florida's business-records exception (§ 90.803(6)(a)) requires the proponent to show: (1) record made at or near the time of the event; (2) by or from information transmitted by a person with knowledge; (3) kept in ordinary course of business; (4) it was regular practice to make such records. The evidentiary proponent bears the burden of laying each element; strict compliance is required.
hearsay_business_records.practical_takeaway: Demand that the debt buyer's trial witness address each Yisrael element; boilerplate 'I am the custodian of records' affidavits without foundational specifics fail the standard.

sol.citation: Fla. Stat. § 95.11(2)(b) (written contract, 5 years); § 95.11(3)(k) (open account, 4 years)
sol.holding_for_defense: Written contracts (including most credit cards and installment loans) carry a 5-year SOL; open accounts carry a 4-year SOL. The SOL runs from the date of first default, not charge-off or sale to the debt buyer.
sol.practical_takeaway: Identify the date of the first missed payment from account statements; if suit was filed more than 5 years after that date, raise SOL as an affirmative defense and evaluate an FDCPA claim for suing on a time-barred debt.

default_vacatur.holding_for_defense: Default judgment entered as a sanction for willful noncompliance with a discovery order was affirmed; standard of review is clear abuse of discretion. The case establishes Florida's framework: a defendant seeking vacatur of a default must show both excusable neglect and a meritorious defense.
default_vacatur.practical_takeaway: When moving to vacate a default judgment under Fla. R. Civ. P. 1.540(b)(1), always pair the excusable-neglect argument with at least two meritorious defenses (SOL, lack of standing, hearsay) to survive the Mercer standard.

default_vacatur.case_name: Fla. R. Civ. P. 1.540(b) — Relief from Judgment
default_vacatur.holding_for_defense: Provides five grounds for relief from final judgment including: excusable neglect (motion within 1 year), newly discovered evidence, fraud/misrepresentation, void judgment (no time limit), and prior judgment reversed.
default_vacatur.practical_takeaway: For improperly-served defendants, argue void judgment under 1.540(b)(4) — no time limit applies and no meritorious defense showing may be required for void judgments.

pleading_requirements.case_name: Fla. R. Civ. P. 1.130 — Instrument Attached
pleading_requirements.citation: Fla. R. Civ. P. 1.130(a) (Jan. 1, 2026 current version)
pleading_requirements.holding_for_defense: All bonds, notes, bills of exchange, contracts, accounts, or documents upon which action may be brought must be incorporated in or attached to the pleading. A complaint based on a written instrument that does not comply fails to state a cause of action.
pleading_requirements.practical_takeaway: File a motion to dismiss immediately after receiving the debt buyer's complaint if the contract or assignment is missing or incomplete; raise separately that the complaint fails to attach the full chain of assignments.

fdcpa_leverage.case_name: Holzman v. Malcolm S. Gerald & Associates, Inc. (LVNV Funding, LLC)
fdcpa_leverage.holding_for_defense: An express threat of litigation is not required to state an FDCPA § 1692e claim with respect to a collection letter seeking payment on a time-barred debt; an implied threat suffices if the letter could deceive or mislead an unsophisticated consumer as to the legal status of the debt.
fdcpa_leverage.practical_takeaway: A settlement-offer letter on a time-barred debt may violate § 1692e even without explicit litigation threats; assert an FDCPA claim whenever a debt buyer attempts to collect after the SOL has expired.

state_counterclaim.citation: Fla. Stat. §§ 559.55–559.785 (Florida Consumer Collection Practices Act)
state_counterclaim.holding_for_defense: The FCCPA applies to original creditors and debt collectors alike (unlike the FDCPA); 19 enumerated prohibited practices; actual damages, statutory damages up to $1,000, and mandatory attorney fees for prevailing consumers.
state_counterclaim.practical_takeaway: Because the FCCPA covers original creditors, it applies to Citibank, Chase, and other original-creditor plaintiffs — not just debt buyers. Stack FCCPA with FDCPA for maximum leverage.

state_counterclaim.case_name: Brook v. Chase Bank USA, N.A. (FCCPA offset issue)
state_counterclaim.holding_for_defense: A bankruptcy court acting within its discretion may refuse to allow a creditor to set off its FCCPA liability against the outstanding debt; the FCCPA is a penal statute designed to deter bad collection practices and allowing setoff would undermine that deterrence.
state_counterclaim.practical_takeaway: Resist any attempt by a plaintiff/counter-defendant to set off FCCPA damages against the underlying debt claim; the deterrent purpose of the statute argues against setoff.

fee_shifting.holding_for_defense: A prevailing consumer in an FCCPA action is entitled to attorney fees and costs; fees are mandatory, not discretionary.
fee_shifting.practical_takeaway: Fee shifting under both FDCPA and FCCPA makes even small-dollar FCCPA violations worth litigating; always plead both statutes together.

standing.holding_for_defense: Discover Bank failed to establish a claim against defendants where it could not produce a signed agreement or itemized proof of card usage; monthly statements alone plus employee affidavits insufficient to show mutual assent to a contract; summary judgment for the bank reversed.
standing.practical_takeaway: Demand the original signed cardmember agreement in discovery; without it, the debt buyer cannot prove mutual assent to the contract terms and the suit should fail on the merits.

standing.holding_for_defense: Debt buyers must be licensed collection agencies and, when filing suit, must attach: the contract or most recent monthly statement, an itemization of post-charge-off additions, the last payment date, and a copy of the assignment or chain-of-title establishing unbroken ownership from original creditor to debt buyer.
standing.practical_takeaway: Any complaint that lacks the chain-of-title assignment exhibits is facially deficient under RCW 19.16.260; move to dismiss and counterclaim for unlicensed collection under RCW 19.16.260 if the plaintiff is not licensed.

chain_of_title.case_name: Bain v. Metropolitan Mortgage Group, Inc.
chain_of_title.holding_for_defense: Only the actual holder of a promissory note or other instrument evidencing the obligation may lawfully exercise rights under the deed of trust act; an entity that does not hold the note lacks standing to foreclose — by analogy, a debt buyer must prove actual ownership of the specific account sued upon.
chain_of_title.practical_takeaway: Use Bain's standing principles by analogy in consumer-debt cases: the plaintiff must show that the specific account was included in each assignment, not just that a bulk portfolio was sold.

chain_of_title.holding_for_defense: If the debt was assigned more than once, each assignment or other writing evidencing transfer of ownership must be attached to establish an unbroken chain of ownership beginning with the original creditor.
chain_of_title.practical_takeaway: Count the number of assignments in the debt buyer's complaint; if any link in the chain is missing, move to dismiss and assert a CPA per se violation for unlicensed or improperly-documented collection.

hearsay_business_records.holding_for_defense: Monthly account statements summarizing alleged balances, without the underlying signed agreement or proof of specific transactions, are insufficient to establish the existence and terms of the contract.
hearsay_business_records.practical_takeaway: Object to account statements as hearsay where no qualified witness can authenticate the original creditor's record-keeping process.

sol.holding_for_defense: Actions upon a written contract or liability arising out of a written agreement must be commenced within six years; accounts receivable (including credit card debts) are also governed by the six-year period.
sol.practical_takeaway: Washington's 6-year SOL is substantially longer than California's 4-year and New York's 3-year SOL; focus SOL defenses on cases approaching or exceeding six years from date of default.

sol.holding_for_defense: Debt buyer could not prove the contractual terms necessary to invoke a Delaware choice-of-law clause; without the signed agreement, the court applied Washington law including Washington's SOL.
sol.practical_takeaway: Challenge Delaware/South Dakota choice-of-law clauses by attacking the debt buyer's ability to prove the contract terms — if the agreement is not authenticated, Washington's 6-year SOL applies.

default_vacatur.case_name: CR 60 — Washington Rules of Civil Procedure
default_vacatur.citation: Wash. Super. Ct. Civil R. 60 (Relief from Judgment)
default_vacatur.holding_for_defense: Washington CR 60 provides relief from final judgment for: (1) mistake/excusable neglect; (4) fraud/misrepresentation; (5) void judgment (no time limit). Washington courts disfavor default judgments and generally grant CR 60 motions where the defendant shows good cause and a meritorious defense.
default_vacatur.practical_takeaway: In Washington, courts bend over backwards to set aside defaults on the merits — pair excusable neglect with specific defenses (SOL, chain of title, unlicensed debt buyer) to maximize the chance of vacatur.

pleading_requirements.holding_for_defense: A debt buyer's complaint must include in no smaller than 10-point type a disclosure that the action is being brought by a person or entity engaged in the business of purchasing delinquent or charged-off claims for collection purposes; failure to include the disclosure is grounds for dismissal.
pleading_requirements.practical_takeaway: Inspect every debt buyer complaint for the RCW 19.16.260 disclosure in ≥ 10-point type; its absence is an independent grounds for dismissal and a per se CPA violation.

fdcpa_leverage.case_name: Panag v. Farmers Insurance Co. of Washington
fdcpa_leverage.holding_for_defense: A CPA claim may be brought by a person who is not in a consumer or other business relationship with the actor; there is no standing barrier preventing a debt-collection target from suing under the CPA. Deceptive characterization of an unadjudicated insurance subrogation claim violates the CPA.
fdcpa_leverage.practical_takeaway: Use Panag to establish CPA standing for any consumer targeted by deceptive collection conduct, even where no prior business relationship existed with the debt buyer.

state_counterclaim.case_name: Hangman Ridge Training Stables, Inc. v. Safeco Title Insurance Co.
state_counterclaim.holding_for_defense: Established the five-element test for a private CPA action: (1) unfair or deceptive act or practice; (2) in trade or commerce; (3) public interest impact; (4) injury to plaintiff's business or property; (5) causation. Per se violations (where the legislature has declared the violation to be a CPA violation) eliminate the need to prove elements 1 and 3.
state_counterclaim.practical_takeaway: Collection Agency Act violations are per se CPA violations; an unlicensed debt buyer or a complaint missing the required disclosures automatically satisfies Hangman Ridge elements 1 and 3.

state_counterclaim.case_name: RCW 19.16.260 Violations = Per Se CPA + CAA Penalties
state_counterclaim.holding_for_defense: Violations of the Collection Agency Act (RCW 19.16) constitute per se CPA violations; a violating debt buyer forfeits all interest, service charges, attorneys' fees, and other charges — only the original principal of the debt may be collected. Additionally, treble damages up to $25,000 and attorney fees are available under RCW 19.86.090.
state_counterclaim.practical_takeaway: Any unlicensed debt buyer or any debt buyer complaint that fails to satisfy RCW 19.16.260 requirements triggers forfeiture of fees and charges plus potential treble-damage liability under the CPA.

fee_shifting.holding_for_defense: A prevailing plaintiff in a private CPA action shall recover the costs of the suit including reasonable attorney fees; treble actual damages up to $25,000 are also available.
fee_shifting.practical_takeaway: The mandatory fee-shift plus treble damages makes CPA/CAA counterclaims highly economically viable; even a client with $2,000 in actual damages could recover $6,000 trebled plus substantial attorney fees.

standing.citation: Unifund CCR, LLC v. Chan (N.Y. Civ. Ct. 2015), cited at 48 Misc.3d 1216(A)
standing.holding_for_defense: Debt buyer could not prove ownership of the assigned debt where its witness lacked personal knowledge of the original creditor's record-keeping practices; records excluded under CPLR § 4518 business-records exception; summary judgment denied.
standing.practical_takeaway: Challenge debt buyer witnesses who cannot testify from personal knowledge about how the originating bank's records were created; 'I received these records' is insufficient foundation under CPLR § 4518.

standing.citation: N.Y. C.P.L.R. § 3016(j) (as amended by Consumer Credit Fairness Act, L.2021, c.593, eff. May 7, 2022)
standing.holding_for_defense: In consumer credit actions, the complaint must include: name of original creditor; last four digits of original account number; date and amount of last payment (or statement that no payment was made); itemization of the amount sought (principal, interest, fees); and a copy of the underlying contract or charge-off statement. Debt buyer plaintiffs must also plead the full chain of title.
standing.practical_takeaway: Check every CCFA complaint against all § 3016(j) requirements on receipt; a complaint missing any element is subject to dismissal and no cure period is automatic.

chain_of_title.case_name: CPLR § 3215 (CCFA default judgment — chain of title)
chain_of_title.citation: N.Y. C.P.L.R. § 3215 (as amended by CCFA, L.2021, c.593)
chain_of_title.holding_for_defense: For a debt buyer to obtain a default judgment in a consumer credit action, it must submit: an affidavit from the original creditor establishing the debt and default; affidavits proving all assignments (full chain of title); and an attorney affidavit stating a reasonable belief that the SOL has not expired.
chain_of_title.practical_takeaway: Oppose any default judgment application by a debt buyer that lacks the full chain-of-title affidavits from each prior assignor — the court must reject the application without them.

chain_of_title.case_name: DLJ Mortgage Capital, Inc. v. United General Title Insurance Co.
chain_of_title.holding_for_defense: Strict interpretation of CPLR 3215's requirements for default judgments: proper service documentation and timely filing are mandatory; attorney verification alone cannot establish merit. Court dismissed where plaintiff's proof was procedurally deficient.
chain_of_title.practical_takeaway: Challenge default judgment applications on procedural grounds under CPLR 3215(f) as well as the substantive CCFA grounds; procedural defects are independently dismissable.

hearsay_business_records.holding_for_defense: Mere submission of billing statements and a minimal partial payment are insufficient to establish account stated; defendants can defeat summary judgment by showing legitimate disputes over charges or identity issues. Personal knowledge requirements for authenticating account records apply.
hearsay_business_records.practical_takeaway: Resist account-stated summary judgments by affidavit of a defendant showing any dispute over the charges; a good-faith dispute defeats the implied-assent theory underlying account stated.

hearsay_business_records.holding_for_defense: Any writing or record must have been made in the regular course of business, at the time of the act or within a reasonable time thereafter; foundation must be established by a witness with personal knowledge of the record-creation process, not merely the custodian who received the records.
hearsay_business_records.practical_takeaway: Cross-examine every debt buyer witness on whether they personally know how the originating bank created and maintained the records they are authenticating — 'I received the file' is insufficient.

sol.case_name: Portfolio Recovery Associates, LLC v. King
sol.holding_for_defense: CPLR § 202 (borrowing statute) may require New York courts to apply the shorter SOL of the state where the cause of action accrued; Delaware's 3-year SOL may bar a credit card suit in New York if the debt accrued while the defendant lived in Delaware — but a choice-of-law clause alone does not import Delaware's SOL without additional showing.
sol.practical_takeaway: Screen every NY case for whether the defendant previously resided in a state with a shorter SOL; if so, research whether CPLR § 202 borrowing shortens the limitations period.

sol.holding_for_defense: Actions arising out of consumer credit transactions where a purchaser, borrower, or debtor is defendant must be commenced within three years. Critically: once the applicable limitations period expires, any subsequent payment toward, written or oral affirmation of, or other activity on the debt does not revive or extend the limitations period — a dramatic change from prior law.
sol.practical_takeaway: The CCFA non-revival provision means that even clients who made a payment on an old debt after the SOL expired are fully protected; the most important SOL defense available in New York consumer debt cases.

default_vacatur.citation: N.Y. C.P.L.R. § 5015 (five grounds for post-judgment relief)
default_vacatur.holding_for_defense: Five grounds for vacatur: (1) excusable default within 1 year of service of notice of entry; (2) newly discovered evidence; (3) fraud/misrepresentation/misconduct; (4) lack of jurisdiction (void judgment, no time limit); (5) reversal of prior judgment. Excusable default requires both a reasonable excuse and a meritorious defense.
default_vacatur.practical_takeaway: For clients who received no service or improper service, challenge jurisdiction under § 5015(a)(4) — no time limit applies and the judgment is void.

pleading_requirements.citation: N.Y. C.P.L.R. §§ 3016(j), 306-d, 3215 (as amended by Consumer Credit Fairness Act, L.2021, c.593)
pleading_requirements.holding_for_defense: CCFA requires: (1) enhanced pleading with original creditor name, account number last four digits, last payment date and amount, itemized balance, and attached contract or charge-off statement; (2) mandatory plain-language notice mailed to defendant within 20 days of filing; (3) enhanced default judgment requirements including full chain-of-title affidavits; failure to comply with any element is grounds for dismissal.
pleading_requirements.practical_takeaway: The CCFA transforms New York's debt-buyer landscape: virtually any CCFA-deficient complaint can be dismissed; file a motion to dismiss on receipt and simultaneously request the mandatory notice required by § 306-d.

fdcpa_leverage.holding_for_defense: FDCPA § 1692e requires debt collectors, when notifying consumers of their account balance, to disclose that the balance may increase due to interest and fees; a collection notice stating only a 'current balance' without this disclosure is false, deceptive, or misleading under the least-sophisticated-consumer standard.
fdcpa_leverage.practical_takeaway: Review every collection letter for the Avila disclosure; a missing 'balance may increase' notice on any letter where interest was accruing is a standalone FDCPA violation.

fdcpa_leverage.case_name: CFPB v. Forster & Garbus LLP (consent order)
fdcpa_leverage.holding_for_defense: Forster & Garbus filed 99,000+ collection lawsuits with meaningful attorney involvement by fewer than a dozen attorneys; court required the firm to possess specific documentation before filing any new lawsuit and to pay a $100,000 civil penalty. Prior CFPB complaint also cited CFPA prohibition on deceptive acts.
fdcpa_leverage.practical_takeaway: Where Forster & Garbus (or any other repeat-filer firm) cannot produce an attorney certification of meaningful involvement in filing the complaint, assert a § 1692e(3) FDCPA violation (false implication of attorney involvement).

state_counterclaim.holding_for_defense: Three-element GBL § 349 claim requires: (1) consumer-oriented conduct; (2) materially misleading in a way likely to deceive a reasonable consumer; (3) plaintiff suffered injury as a result. No reliance requirement; causation is sufficient. Statutory minimum $50 per violation; treble actual damages at court discretion; mandatory attorney fees.
state_counterclaim.practical_takeaway: GBL § 349 is ideal for deceptive collection letters, misleading claim amounts, and false representation of legal status — no reliance needed, just causation from the deceptive act.

state_counterclaim.case_name: Oswego Laborers' Local 214 Pension Fund v. Marine Midland Bank
state_counterclaim.holding_for_defense: GBL § 349 consumer-oriented conduct does not require a pattern of repetitive deceptive behavior; the conduct must have a broader impact on consumers at large in the sense that it 'potentially affects similarly situated consumers'; institutional conduct that is consumer-facing qualifies.
state_counterclaim.practical_takeaway: Debt buyer collection practices are paradigmatically 'consumer-oriented' under Oswego — the same letters are sent to thousands of consumers, satisfying the public-impact element.

state_counterclaim.case_name: 23 N.Y.C.R.R. Part 1 (NY DFS Debt Collection Regulation)
state_counterclaim.citation: 23 N.Y.C.R.R. §§ 1.1–1.7 (DFS Debt Collection Regulation, eff. March 3, 2015)
state_counterclaim.holding_for_defense: DFS regulation requires debt collectors to substantiate debts within 60 days of a written request; failure is actionable under GBL § 601 and the DFS regulation; Forster & Garbus charged with multiple violations in 2020 DFS enforcement action.
state_counterclaim.practical_takeaway: Send a written substantiation request within the first 30 days of any representation; if the debt buyer fails to respond within 60 days, document the violation and assert it under GBL § 601.

fee_shifting.holding_for_defense: A prevailing plaintiff under GBL § 349 is entitled to actual damages or $50, whichever is greater; treble actual damages at court discretion; reasonable attorney fees. Fee award is mandatory if the plaintiff prevails.
fee_shifting.practical_takeaway: GBL § 349 combined with FDCPA generates layered fee-shifting; document all attorney time meticulously from Day 1 for the lodestar calculation.

fee_shifting.holding_for_defense: There is no per se rule against awarding fees in excess of damages recovered; fees may be appropriate even where a party recovers only nominal damages. Court affirmed a $4 million fee award on $1.5 million in actual damages.
fee_shifting.practical_takeaway: Do not accept the argument that attorney fees must be proportionate to damages recovered; Seibel supports large fee awards even on modest actual-damage recoveries.
